step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT -> SPACE -> RESET

sections.content:

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 2 has two diagonal red blocks. SPACE swaps player with adjacent red block, can merge them. Level 1 was solved by moving to center between red blocks and pressing SPACE. Let me try the same pattern for level 2.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 94 actions; a strong score needs about 58 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 25 actions returned the board to a state already seen 25 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 25 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 4 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game has reset. Let me try a completely different approach. Instead of trying to merge the red blocks, let me try to move the player to a position that mirrors the level 1 solution.
In level 1, the player moved DOWN 3, RIGHT 3 from the initial position and pressed SPACE. The initial position was rows 24-27, cols 8-27. After DOWN 3, RIGHT 3, the player was at rows 36-39, cols 20-39.
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me reconsider.
Actually, in level 1, the initial position was rows 24-27, cols 8-27. The player moved DOWN 3, RIGHT 3 to rows 36-39, cols 20-39. Then SPACE completed the level.
In level 2, the initial position is rows 36-39, cols 20-39. This is the same position as the level 1 completion position!
So maybe in level 2, the player needs to move to a different position. Let me think about what position would make sense.
Actually, wait. In level 1, the player started at rows 24-27, cols 8-27 and moved to rows 36-39, cols 20-39. In level 2, the player starts at rows 36-39, cols 20-39. Maybe the player needs to move back to rows 24-27, cols 8-27 and press SPACE.
Let me try UP 3, LEFT 3, SPACE.
But I already tried this before, and it swapped the player with red 2. Let me check if this is the correct approach.
Actually, in level 1, the player moved DOWN 3, RIGHT 3, SPACE. The red blocks in level 1 were at rows 8-11, cols 24-35 and rows 16-19, cols 40-51. The player moved to rows 36-39, cols 20-39, which was below and to the right of the red blocks.
In level 2, the red blocks are at rows 16-19, cols 8-19 and rows 24-27, cols 28-39. The player is at rows 36-39, cols 20-39, which is below the red blocks.
Maybe the goal in level 2 is to move the player to a position that's "between" the red blocks in some sense. The red blocks form a diagonal from top-left to bottom-right. The position between them would be around rows 20-23, cols 20-39.
Let me try UP 5, SPACE. This would move the player to rows 20-23, cols 20-39.
Actually wait, I need to be more careful. The player is 4x20. From rows 36-39, cols 20-39:
- UP 1: rows 32-35
- UP 2: rows 28-31
- UP 3: rows 24-27
- UP 4: rows 20-23
- UP 5: rows 16-19
So UP 4 would put the player at rows 20-23, cols 20-39. This is between the two red blocks (red 1 at rows 16-19, cols 8-19 and red 2 at rows 24-27, cols 28-39).
Let me try UP 4, SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving to position between red blocks and pressing SPACE
# Player at rows 36-39, cols 20-39
# UP 4 → rows 20-23, cols 20-39 (between red blocks)
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
